column4: What is the candidate's name as it will appear on the ballot?
column6: Please describe any professional and/or life experiences that will help voters assess your qualifications for the office you’re seeking.
column7: Where do you live currently? Where (city and state) were you born? How long have you lived in Montana?
column8: Age as of Nov. 3, 2026, date of birth, and place of birth. We will not publish your birth date.
column9: What other biographical information do you want voters to know about you?
column10: (Optional) Please submit a high-resolution photo of yourself in this field. Alternately, you can send an electronic copy of your candidate photo to tlutey@montanafreepress.org.
column13: What should be the PSC's role, if any, in regulating data center development in Montana?
column14: How should the PSC approach the NorthWestern/Black Hills Corp. merger?
column15: What is one industry under the PSC's purview that could use more scrutiny than it's currently getting, and what might that increased scrutiny look like?

column6: I'm a CPA/Accountant and the ratemaking process is very focused on Profit & Loss Statements and Balance Sheets. I'm well positioned to turn a sharp eye to the books while making rate price decisions. I've also been a Legislator for what will be 8 years at the end of my term this year.
column7: I live in Great Falls. I was born in Missoula. I've lived in Montana for 41 years (I'm 41).
column8: I'll be 42 on Nov 3, 2026. My DOB is 06/03/1984, and was born in Missoula, MT.
column9: I'm married to Hannah, our 21st anniversary is coming up in July. I have four kids, ages 18, 16, 14, and 5 months old. In my professional life I've been an accountant at a nonprofit, corporate healthcare, and run my own small business (tax prep). I've owned residential rentals and commercial property which inform my thinking on property taxes and utility costs like gas and electric.
column13: The PSC should work with poliymakers cooperatively to form laws that have data centers "bring their own generation" or pay for their own power distribution infrastructure. The point being that data centers should not be allowed to increase electric rates because of extra invested assets being put into the rate base, so if they will be a burden to other ratepayers, policies should control for that.
column14: The PSC should follow the law and perform due diligence to decide whether the merger is in the best interest of ratepayers. The Montana PSC is not the only state regulator looking at this and each of the state regulatory commissions should work to ensure the deal is best for most constiuents. If the deal makes sense and produces economies of scale, and has a high potential to meet capacity needs, then it could work out well for ratepayers.
column15: The energy industry does get a lot of attention, so second to that, the waste hauling business is important to keep a keen eye on as well. It is a large industry and competition is limited. Adequate service at reasonable prices would be my goal and focus when regulating this area under the PSC's purview.
